title: FINRA Case 25-02055 — Stephen Wedel v. LPL Financial LLC
text:
Expungement Award
FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
In the Matter of the Arbitration Between:
Claimant Case Number: 25-02055
Stephen Wedel
vs.
Respondent Hearing Site: Boca Raton, Florida
LPL Financial LLC
Awards are rendered by independent arbitrators who are appointed pursuant to Rule 13806 of
the Code of Arbitration Procedure (“Code”) to issue final, binding decisions. FINRA makes
available an arbitration forum—pursuant to rules approved by the SEC—but has no part in
deciding the award.
Nature of the Dispute: Associated Person vs. Member
The expungement hearing was conducted by videoconference.
REPRESENTATION OF PARTIES
For Claimant Stephen Wedel (“Claimant”): Chemere K. Ellis, Esq. and Kristen Rickard, Esq.,
Chemere Ellis, PLLC, Tampa, Florida.
For Respondent LPL Financial LLC (“Respondent”): Michael T. West, Esq., LPL Financial LLC,
San Diego, California.
CASE INFORMATION
Statement of Claim filed on or about: September 26, 2025.
Claimant signed the Submission Agreement: September 26, 2025.
Statement of Answer filed on or about: October 22, 2025.
Respondent signed the Submission Agreement: October 22, 2025.
CASE SUMMARY
In the Statement of Claim, Claimant asserted a claim seeking expungement of customer dispute
information from registration records maintained by the Central Registration Depository (“CRD”).
In the Statement of Answer, Respondent did not oppose Claimant’s expungement request.

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02055
Award Page 2 of 8
RELIEF REQUESTED
In the Statement of Claim, Claimant (CRD Number 1221992) requested expungement of all
references to Occurrence Numbers 2088432, 2208294, and 2337268 from the CRD registration
records.
In the Statement of Answer, Respondent did not delineate any specific relief request.
OTHER ISSUES CONSIDERED AND DECIDED
The Arbitrators acknowledge that they have each read the pleadings and other materials filed by
the parties.
On October 21, 2025, Claimant advised that the customers in Occurrence Numbers 2088432
(“Customer A”), 2208294 (“Customers B”), and 2337268 (“Customers C”), hereinafter
collectively referred to as the “Customers,” were served with a copy of the Statement of Claim,
although not within 10 days of filing the Statement of Claim and provided proof of service.
On February 2, 2026, Claimant advised that the Customers were served with a copy of the
Statement of Answer, although not within 10 days of filing the Statement of Answer, and
provided proof of service.
The Panel conducted a recorded hearing by videoconference on March 12, 2026, so the parties
could present oral argument and evidence on Claimant’s request for expungement. Claimant
appeared by videoconference at the expungement hearing. Respondent appeared at the
expungement hearing.
The Customers did not appear at the expungement hearing.
No authorized representative of a state securities regulator appeared at the expungement hearing.
The Panel noted Claimant’s representation that a prior arbitration panel or court has not
previously ruled on expungement of the same occurrences in the CRD.
The Panel reviewed the settlement documentation related to Occurrence Numbers 2088432,
2208294, and 2337268, considered the amount of payment made to any party to the
settlements, and considered other relevant terms and conditions of the settlements. The Panel
noted that the settlements were not conditioned on any party to the settlement not opposing the
expungement request and that Claimant did not contribute to the settlement amounts.
AWARD
After considering the pleadings, the testimony and evidence presented at the expungement
hearing, and any post-hearing submissions, the Panel has decided in full and final resolution of
the issues submitted for determination as follows:

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02055
Award Page 3 of 8
1. Claimant’s request for expungement of Occurrence Number 2088432 from registration
records maintained by the CRD is denied with prejudice. The Panel’s decision was
unanimous.
The Panel is not awarding expungement with respect to Occurrence Number 2088432 for
the reasons stated below. On about August 20, 2020, Customer A brought an arbitration
against Respondent pertaining to two investments of $107,940.00 each purchased on the
recommendation of Claimant as registered representative: NorthStar Health Income Trust
and Franklin Square Investment Corporation III.
The Panel concludes that none of the grounds for expungement are met with respect to
Customer A’s two investments. In particular, the Panel is not able to conclude that
allegations of Customer A’s complaint are either clearly erroneous or false. The reasons
are set forth below.
While Claimant is not named as a party in Customer A’s Statement of Claim, did not
participate in the defense or settlement, and did not contribute to the settlement,
Customer A’s Statement of Claim is full of allegations of wrongdoing by Claimant,
including failure to recommend suitable investments, breach of fiduciary duty, negligent
misrepresentations or omissions and fraud. The matter settled for $145,000.00, an
amount that represents 67% of the total cost of the two investments Claimant
recommended.
Customer A was a 55-year-old widower at the time of the purchases, his wife having
passed away that year. Customer A had completed some college courses and had
operated a landscape business since 1985. In contrast to Claimant’s assertions
concerning Customer A’s sophistication for securities investment purposes, the
Statement of Claim alleged Customer A had a very limited understanding of securities in
general and of the non-traded alternative investments Claimant recommended. The funds
available for investment were $216,000.00 in life insurance proceeds from Customer A’s
wife’s death, which Customer A wanted to invest to produce income so that he could fund
vacations for himself and his daughters. This objective seems inconsistent with the high
risk alternative investments purchased.
According to the subscription agreements, Customer A had an annual income of
$100,000.00 and a liquid net worth of $1,279,045.00. The two recommended investments
represented 17% of Customer A’s net worth. Moreover, because Customer A already had
in his portfolio two alternative investments with a total purchase price of $38,138.00, the
additional alternative investments brought alternative investments in his portfolio to nearly
20%, which seems overly concentrated given Customer A’s circumstances and his
objectives. Although Claimant stated that he had explained to Customer A the essential
features and risks of the investments and the consequences of the selection of a
“Growth” objective, Claimant offered little to no documentation in the form of letters to his
client, contemporaneous notes, or the like in support of his position. Moreover, as
Claimant acknowledged in his declaration, in Customer A’s Statement of Claim,
Customer A’s objectives are characterized as more “moderate.” The evidence is
insufficient to conclude that Customer A was more risk-oriented or that his arbitration
position was clearly erroneous or false. Documents upon which the Panel relied in

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02055
Award Page 4 of 8
coming to this conclusion include: Exhibit 1 (Statement of Claim for Customer A); Exhibit
4 (meeting information sheet); Composite Exhibit 5 (subscription agreements and
alternate investment purchase forms); Composite Exhibit 2 (Customer A’s account
application forms, and Exhibit I (Customer A’s settlement agreement).
2. The Panel awards the expungement of all references to Occurrence Numbers 2208294 and
2337268 from registration records maintained by the CRD for Claimant Stephen Herman
Wedel (CRD Number 1221992) with the understanding that, pursuant to Rule 2080,
Claimant Stephen Herman Wedel must obtain confirmation from a court of competent
jurisdiction before the CRD will execute the expungement directive.
Unless specifically waived in writing by FINRA, parties seeking judicial confirmation of an
arbitration award containing customer dispute expungement relief must name FINRA as an
additional party and serve FINRA with all appropriate documents.
Pursuant to Rule 13805 of the Code, the Panel has made the following affirmative finding of
fact:
The claim, allegation, or information is factually impossible or clearly erroneous.
The Panel has made the above finding based on the following reasons:
Occurrence Number 2208294
The Panel concludes that Occurrence Number 2208294 should be expunged as clearly
erroneous. On about May 16, 2022, Customers B initiated an arbitration against
Respondent. Claimant was not named as a party (although he was referenced and
Customers B complained of his actions), did not participate in the defense or settlement
of the claim, and was not asked to contribute to the settlement. The arbitration was
settled for $8,000.00, an amount that seems substantially less than the cost of defense
and consistent with nuisance value.
On May 15, 2014, Customers B purchased an interest in Franklin Square Energy and
Power for $133,334.00. (Customers B also purchased an interest in WP Carey CPA18
("CPA 18"), a similarly risky alternative investment, for $133,334.00, an interest about
which Customers B did not complain.)
The Panel is persuaded by credible evidence of the following:
Customers B were unquestionably experienced investors. One of Customers B was a
Certified Public Accountant ("CPA") with substantial experience in accounting and
business and held a senior position with Arch Coal, a major U.S. firm. Customers B were
active investors in stocks and bonds. They identified themselves as aggressive investors;
they identified growth as their sole investment objective and indicated that they had a
high tolerance for risk. Customers B reported their annual income as $109,450.00, and
their liquid net worth as $1,625,500.00. Claimant testified that they told him they were
interested in diversifying their portfolio with additional alternative investments and also
that they had no liquidity needs for the funds they were investing. Customers B expressly

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02055
Award Page 5 of 8
acknowledged the risks of the investment. Customers B had conversations with Claimant
in which they reviewed Customers B’s assets and accounts, their background, goals and
investment objectives. Claimant provided Customers B with written disclosure materials
about the investment, including the prospectus, subscription agreement, and
Respondent's alternative investment purchase forms. They also discussed the essential
features and associated risks of the investment. The disclosures were confirmed by
letters from Claimant corroborating the investment choices.
Based on the foregoing, the Panel finds that Customers B’s claim was clearly erroneous.
These findings are supported by the testimony of Claimant at the hearing and his
declaration (Exhibit D); Exhibit 2 (Customers B’s statement of claim); Exhibits 6 and 7
(confirming letters from Claimant to Customers B, and Customers B’s text string);
Composite Exhibit 3 (account applications); Exhibit 4 (FSEP subscription agreement and
alternative investment purchase form); Exhibit 5 (CPA 18 global order form dated May 5,
2015); and Exhibit J (the settlement agreement).
Occurrence Number 2337268
The Panel concludes that Occurrence Number 2337268 should be expunged as clearly
erroneous. On about May 2, 2024, Customers C (together with others) brought an
arbitration against Respondent complaining of the following alternative investment
purchases: an interest in Cole Credit Property Trust IV ("Cole Credit IV") in November
2013 for $152,292.00; an interests in NorthStar Health Care Income Trust ("NorthStar")
purchased in November 2013 and January 2014 for a total of $90,200.00, and an interest
in Franklin Square Energy and Power ("FSEP") purchased October 27, 2014 for
$36,700.00. For the reasons set forth below, the Panel concludes that Customers C’s
claim should be expunged from Claimant’s CRD records under FINRA Rule 13805 as
clearly erroneous. The reasons for this conclusion are as follows:
Claimant was not named as a party in the Statement of Claim ("SOC"), did not participate
in the defense or settlement and was not asked to contribute to the settlement. The SOC
is very general, extremely brief, does not mention Claimant and focuses entirely on the
legal responsibilities of Respondent and appears to involve a large number of similarly
situated investors who purchased alternative investments through Respondent.
Customers C’s settlement portion was $28,000.00. For Customers C, the settlement
represents about 10% of the sum of the purchase prices for the alternative investments
they complain of. This amount is somewhat difficult to assess in the context of a group
action, but is a low percentage that does not support a high value claim. Claimant
credibly testified that Customers C had been his clients since 2006, some 7 to 8 years
before the investments in question were made. During that time Claimant obtained
information about their background, finances, investing experience and goals. Customers
C described themselves as moderate to moderately aggressive investors. In 2012, when
they opened their accounts with Respondent they put their annual income at $100,000.00
and their liquid net worth at $1,522,921.00. They had a 10-to-15-year investment horizon
and identified their sole investment objective as "growth." They stated that they had 20+
years of investment experience in stocks, and also had exposure to alternative
investments, having purchased approximately $220,000.00 in such investments--Island

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02055
Award Page 6 of 8
American REIT and Cole Credit Property Trust III--prior to engaging Claimant. Claimant
testified that Customers C told him that they wanted to steer clear of investments tied to
the stock market because of their experience during the 2008 market decline and
volatility and to continue to diversify their portfolio with alternative investments.
Customers C were investors qualified to choose to invest in the alternative investments
recommended to them. Claimant recommended that Customers C purchase Cole Credit
IV (a sister to Cole Credit III which Customers C owned and liked), NorthStar and FSEP.
Based on Customers C’s financial profile, investing experience and objectives, and the
characteristics of the investments, the investments were suitable for Customers C.
Customers C received adequate disclosures about the proposed investments. Claimant
provided prospectuses and reviewed the features of the investments with them. Claimant
sent confirmatory, corroborative letters to Customers C.
Based on the foregoing, the Panel finds that Customers C’s claim was clearly erroneous.
These findings are supported by the credible testimony of Claimant and his declaration
(Exhibit D); Composite Exhibit 8 (confirmatory letters); Composite Exhibit 9 (subscription
agreements for the three investments and the alternative investment purchase forms);
Exhibit C (Statement of Claim against Respondent); Exhibit 6 (confidential client profile
dated September 18, 2006); Composite Exhibit 7 (account application); Exhibit K
(settlement agreement).
FEES
Pursuant to the Code, the following fees are assessed:
Filing Fees
FINRA Dispute Resolution Services assessed a filing fee* for each claim:
Initial Claim Filing Fee =$ 2,000.00
*The filing fee is made up of a non-refundable and a refundable portion.
Member Fees
Member fees are assessed to each member firm that is a party in these proceedings or to the
member firm(s) that employed the associated person(s) at the time of the event(s) giving rise to
the dispute. Accordingly, as a party, Respondent is assessed the following:
Member Surcharge =$ 2,600.00
Member Process Fee =$ 5,005.00
Hearing Session Fees and Assessments
The Panel has assessed hearing session fees for each session conducted. A session is any
meeting between the parties and the Arbitrator(s), including a pre-hearing conference with the
Arbitrator(s), which lasts four (4) hours or less. Fees associated with these proceedings are:
One (1) hearing session on expungement request @ $1,725.00/session =$ 1,725.00
Hearing: March 12, 2026 1 session

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02055
Award Page 7 of 8
Total Hearing Session Fees =$ 1,725.00
The Panel has assessed the total hearing session fees to Claimant.
All balances are payable to FINRA Dispute Resolution Services and are due upon receipt.

FINRA Dispute Resolution Services
Arbitration No. 25-02055
Award Page 8 of 8
ARBITRATION PANEL
Katherine Hendricks - Public Arbitrator, Presiding Chairperson
Murray S. Levin - Public Arbitrator
Alice E. Winkler - Public Arbitrator
I, the undersigned Arbitrator, do hereby affirm that I am the individual described herein and who
executed this instrument, which is my award.
Concurring Arbitrators' Signatures
